1:
As Jesus leaves the temple and the disciples point to its buildings, the cloud of witnesses reads the moment as his final departure and the prelude to the temple’s ruin. The historical and allegorical readings run side by side.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson, citing Josephus, describe the enormous polished stones and the beauty of Herod’s still-unfinished temple (John 2:20); Robertson calls it ‘a tragic moment,’ Jesus leaving the sacred enclosure ‘for good’ after the denunciation of Matthew 23. Lightfoot quotes the rabbis on the marble’s splendour. Ellicott thinks the disciples’ act follows on the ominous words just heard, half-hoping to avert the doom.

The fathers read both history and figure. Raban states the plain sense — the city and temple were overthrown in the forty-second year after the passion under Vespasian and Titus; Remigius adds that the temple was removed lest weak believers be drawn back to ‘carnal Judaism.’ Jerome and Origen allegorise: when the Lord departs, ‘all the buildings of the Law’ fall, and each man, being God’s temple (compare 1 Corinthians 3:16), is ruined when his sin drives Christ away. Chrysostom presses the ‘not one stone’ to its literal completeness.

The Reformed Calvin and the Puritan Henry draw the warning. Calvin sees the disciples ‘dazzled by the splendour,’ scarcely able to believe Christ’s kingdom could rise while so vast a pile stood; he likens it to those who admire the wealth and pomp of Popery and despise a mean-looking Church. Henry adds that ‘that house is left desolate indeed which Christ leaves’ (compare Hosea 9:12), and that ‘a believing foresight of the defacing of all worldly glory’ should wean us from overvaluing it. The shared note is that the temple’s doom, however incredible, is certain, and its very grandeur must not blind faith to the kingdom Christ is about to build. Henry adds that three days later the temple veil was rent, and that the prediction includes the period of the Levitical priesthood and ceremonial law; the witnesses thus see in the doom of the buildings a token that the whole shadow-economy was passing away, the true glory of the latter house being Christ himself (Haggai 2:9).
2:
‘There shall not be left one stone upon another.’ The witnesses agree this was literally and astonishingly fulfilled in A.D. 70, and they marshal Josephus and the rabbis as unwitting commentators on the prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the fulfilment. Barnes notes that at the time nothing was more improbable — the temple was ‘vast, rich, splendid… the nation was at peace’ — yet within forty years Titus took the city, and though he wished to spare the temple, the soldiers burnt it ‘against the will of Caesar.’ Clarke and Lightfoot add from the Talmud and Maimonides that Turnus (Terentius) Rufus ploughed up the foundations, fulfilling Micah 3:12, ‘Zion shall be ploughed as a field.’ Ellicott observes that the remains since disinterred belong only to the substructures. Robertson sees the gloomy prediction unchanged by the disciples’ admiration.

The Reformed Calvin explains the purpose: the vast wealth of the temple ‘hung like a veil before the eyes of the disciples,’ so Christ swears with an oath that it will quickly perish, both to free them from undue attachment to its shadows and because ‘God determined, by this dreadful example, to take vengeance on that nation, for having rejected his Son.’ Wesley notes the foundations were dug up after the burning.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem here expounds the two names ‘Jesus Christ’ and the Joshua typology, and so does not bear on the verse. The shared, dominant note is that the temple’s utter ruin — not a stone left, the very ground ploughed — both sealed the truth of Christ’s word and marked the end of the Levitical priesthood and the whole ceremonial economy, God’s vengeance on a nation that had despised its Redeemer. Clarke draws out a threefold reason for the ruin: the temple was destroyed justly, for the nation’s sins; mercifully, to take away the occasion of continuing in Judaism; and mysteriously, to show the ancient sacrifices abolished and the whole Jewish economy ended. The witnesses agree that what at the time seemed wholly incredible — the levelling of so vast and strong a pile in a time of peace — became one of the clearest proofs of the truth of Christ’s word, fulfilled to the letter within a single generation.
3:
On the Mount of Olives the disciples ask three questions — when these things shall be, the sign of Christ’s coming, and the end of the world. The witnesses agree the disciples confusedly ran the three together, and the chapter’s whole interpretive problem opens here.

The grammatical-historical commentators frame the questions. Barnes holds there are three, answered by intermingling the destruction of Jerusalem and the end of the world, since both ‘could be described in the same words’ (compare the double reference in Isaiah 13). Clarke leans preterist, taking ‘the end of the world’ (better, ‘the age,’ aion) as the Jewish economy. Ellicott and Robertson note this is the first occurrence of parousia (‘presence,’ compare 1 Thessalonians 2:19); Robertson’s famous image is of a painter blending on one canvas the room within, the field without, and the sky beyond — Jesus blends the fall of Jerusalem, his second coming, and the end of the age.

The fathers distinguish the strands. Chrysostom and Jerome count three questions; Hilary says they are ‘separated by different times and meanings,’ the destruction of the city answered first. Remigius and Origen allegorise the Mount of Olives as the Church of the Gentiles, fruitful with the oil of mercy. Cyril of Jerusalem, whose whole lecture is on the Second Coming, reads the question as a providential safeguard against being deceived by Antichrist.

The Reformed Calvin diagnoses the disciples’ error: bred to believe the temple would stand till the end, they could not imagine its fall without ‘the end of the world,’ and so ‘leap at once to various questions,’ confounding the commencement of Christ’s reign with its consummation, and grasping at triumph before warfare. Wesley sets out the structure: Christ answers the temple’s destruction first, then his coming and the end. The shared note is that the disciples’ tangled question draws a layered answer. Lightfoot sets the question against the Jewish expectation of the days of Messiah, when the righteous, once raised, would never return to dust; and Robertson notes that parousia, here first used in the Gospels, recurs through the epistles for Christ’s second coming. The witnesses agree that the disciples’ tangled question called forth a layered answer that has become the most difficult discourse in the Synoptic Gospels.
4:
‘Take heed that no man deceive you.’ The witnesses agree Christ’s first word is a caution against deceivers, and they unfold the principle by which the whole discourse is to be read.

The Puritan Henry states the key: the prophecy ‘primarily respects… the destruction of Jerusalem… but as the prophecies of the Old Testament… under the figure… look further, to the gospel church and the kingdom of the Messiah, so this prophecy, under the type of Jerusalem’s destruction, looks as far forward’ as the end of the world. This double-reference principle governs how most of the witnesses handle the chapter.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the warning. Barnes and Clarke make the first sign ‘false Christs,’ many laying claim to the Messiahship that was then universally expected (Matthew 2:2). Robertson notes the verb ‘lead you astray’ (whence our word ‘planet’) runs through the discourse: ‘it is amazing how successful deceivers have been… with their eschatological programs.’ Ellicott observes the discourse, reported from memory, probably came first from Peter’s lips (compare 1 Thessalonians 5:2).

The Reformed Calvin draws the pastoral aim: the disciples, eager for triumph, ‘must first finish the course’; the preaching of the gospel is like sowing seed, and we must ‘wait patiently for the time of reaping,’ not losing courage at the frosts of winter. The two charges Christ presses are to beware false teachers and not to be terrified by scandals. Wesley adds that the caution is ‘more particularly designed for the succeeding Christians.’ Cyril of Jerusalem, with the futurist fathers, applies it to the final Antichrist. The shared note is vigilance: the times of upheaval breed deceivers, and faith must be neither deluded nor dismayed. Barnes stresses that Christ requires constant watchfulness, since many would arise and many be easily led, the Messiah being then universally expected. The witnesses agree that the whole discourse is governed by Henry’s principle of double reference, so that warnings spoken first of Jerusalem’s fall reach forward, as types, to the church and the end of the age; vigilance against deception and steadiness against alarm are the two charges that run through it from beginning to end.
5:
‘Many shall come in my name, saying, I am Christ, and shall deceive many.’ The witnesses agree this is the first sign — false Messiahs — and most document its fulfilment before A.D. 70.

The grammatical-historical commentators name the impostors. Clarke and Robertson, citing Josephus, list the many who ‘pretending to Divine inspiration… deceived the people,’ leading multitudes into the desert — Simon Magus (Acts 8:9), Dositheus, Theudas, the Egyptian (Acts 21:38). Robertson notes they would ‘arrogate… false claims of Messiahship’ in Christ’s name. Ellicott is more cautious: no direct Messianic pretender is named by Josephus before the fall (Bar-Cochba came c. A.D. 120), though the ‘many antichrists’ of 1 John 2:18 may point to such figures.

The Reformed Calvin explains the providence: shortly after the resurrection ‘there arose impostors, every one of whom professed to be’ the redeemer, and God ‘permitted such reveries to impose on the Jews, who had so basely rejected his Son,’ that they might be driven to despair and destruction. He warns believers not to faint at seeing a multitude carried away, ‘especially when we are few in number.’

The Wesleyan Wesley observes that ‘never did so many impostors appear… as a few years before the destruction of Jerusalem,’ because that was when the Jews generally expected the Messiah. Cyril of Jerusalem notes the saying ‘has happened in part’ in Simon and Menander, and will again. The shared, dominant note is that the rise of false Christs, deceiving many, was both a sign of Jerusalem’s approaching ruin and a standing pattern of the deceptions faith must resist. Gill explains that these would come ‘in my name,’ assuming the Messiah’s office and dignity though delegated by none; and Clarke adds that it was ‘a just judgment’ that the nation which rejected the true Christ should be delivered up to false ones. The witnesses agree that the rise of these pretenders both signalled Jerusalem’s approaching ruin and set the enduring pattern of deceptions the church must resist, so that believers are not to faint though they see a multitude carried away. Cyril of Jerusalem confirms that the saying had already begun to be fulfilled in his own day, and would be again.
6:
‘Ye shall hear of wars and rumours of wars… but the end is not yet.’ The witnesses agree this second sign refers chiefly to the commotions before Jerusalem’s fall, and that Christ steadies his people against premature alarm.

The grammatical-historical commentators document the wars. Barnes notes that at the time the empire was at peace, yet four emperors (Nero, Galba, Otho, Vitellius) soon died violently and civil war spread; Clarke and Ellicott add the threatened wars under Caligula and Claudius. Robertson dwells on ‘see that ye be not troubled’ (the same verb Paul uses in 2 Thessalonians 2:2): ‘it is curious how people overlook these words… and proceed to set dates for the immediate end.’

The fathers read both history and figure. Chrysostom takes the wars of Jerusalem and the providential pestilences and earthquakes as ‘wrath from heaven.’ Augustine sets the whole discourse under his threefold key — Jerusalem, Christ’s coming through the Church, and the final judgment — warning that we ‘carefully consider which signs belong to which events.’ Origen allegorises the convulsions as the world sickening before its end; Jerome reads the strife as that of heretics. Raban notes the warning kept the apostles from fleeing Judea too soon.

The Reformed Calvin stresses providence: none of these things happen ‘without the providence of God,’ and Christ bids the disciples prepare their minds for endurance rather than promise themselves ease. Wesley distinguishes ‘wars’ (near) from ‘rumours’ (at a distance), and Cyril of Jerusalem points to the Persian-Roman wars of his own day. The shared note is that upheaval is the beginning, not the end — a summons to calm endurance, not to date-setting. Augustine’s threefold key — that the discourse speaks of Jerusalem, of Christ’s coming through the Church, and of the final judgment — bids us weigh carefully which signs belong to which event, lest we refer to one what belongs to another. The witnesses agree that wars and rumours are the beginning, not the end, a summons to calm watchfulness rather than to the date-setting that overlooks Christ’s plain ‘the end is not yet.’ Calvin insists that none of these commotions fall out apart from the providence of God, and that Christ bids his people brace for endurance rather than promise themselves ease.
7:
‘Nation shall rise against nation… famines, and pestilences, and earthquakes in divers places.’ The witnesses, chiefly grammatical-historical, document each calamity from the years before A.D. 70.

The grammatical-historical commentators marshal the evidence. Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott, citing Josephus, recount the bloody conflicts between Jews and Syrians at Caesarea (twenty thousand slain), Scythopolis, Alexandria and elsewhere, and the civil war of Otho and Vitellius. For famine they point to that foretold by Agabus (Acts 11:28), severe under Claudius; Ellicott notes the four famines of his reign. For pestilence, plagues at Rome and Babylon — though Ellicott observes the word is text-critically doubtful here, perhaps drawn from Luke 21:11 (the Greek limos/loimos easily confused). Earthquakes, they add, marked the period as few others.

Lightfoot heightens the picture from Tacitus: the commotions of Otho, Vitellius and Vespasian so shook the empire that ‘the fortune of the empire changed with the change of the whole world,’ Rome itself becoming a battlefield and the Capitol burnt — ‘the scourge and vengeance of God upon the Jews.’

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is on point, asking whether ‘at this time’ there is not war and famine and earthquake, and bidding men watch. The shared, dominant note is that the convulsions Christ foretold — political, natural, and providential — were strikingly verified in the generation before Jerusalem fell, and so confirm both the truth of his word and the call to vigilance; yet, as the previous verse insisted, they are signs of the beginning, not proofs that the end has come. Clarke recounts the mutual slaughters — Jews and Syrians at Caesarea, Scythopolis, Ascalon, Ptolemais, Alexandria and Damascus, tens of thousands falling on either side — and the wars of tetrarchy against tetrarchy. Barnes notes that in prophetic language earthquakes may also denote political commotions, though literal tremors abounded in the period. The witnesses agree that these convulsions were so strikingly verified in the years before the fall that Josephus reads almost like a running commentary on Christ’s words. Lightfoot heightens the picture from Tacitus, the empire so shaken that Rome itself became a battlefield and the Capitol was burnt — the very scourge and vengeance of God upon the Jews.
8:
‘All these are the beginning of sorrows.’ The witnesses agree the phrase means birth-pangs, and that the calamities so far named are only the prelude to far heavier woes.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the metaphor. Barnes says plainly, ‘far heavier calamities are yet to come before the end.’ Clarke pictures ‘the whole land of Judea… under the notion of a woman in grievous travail,’ these being only ‘the first pangs… nothing in comparison of that… death-bringing labour’ to follow. Ellicott and Robertson note the word means ‘travail pangs,’ which the Jews used for ‘the sorrows of the Messiah’ that must precede his coming; the troubles issue in a ‘new birth’ — the regeneration of Matthew 19:28, the whole creation travailing in pain together (Romans 8:22). Robertson stresses they are ‘not a proof of the end, but of the beginning.’

Gill likewise records that the Jews called these woes ‘the sorrows of the Messiah’ and longed to be delivered from them. The Reformed Calvin draws the pastoral point: not that believers, who ‘always have abundant consolations in calamities,’ should consume themselves with grief, but that they should ‘lay their account with a long exercise of patience.’

The shared, dominant note is that the wars, famines, and earthquakes are the first throes of a longer travail, designed to brace the disciples for endurance rather than to alarm them with the nearness of the end. The image of birth-pangs holds out hope as well as warning: the sorrows are real and will deepen, but they are the labour that ushers in a new order, and so the people of God are to meet them with patience and consolation, not despair. Gill records that the Jews themselves called these woes ‘the sorrows of the Messiah’ and longed to be delivered from them, and Ellicott likens a time of national perplexity to the travail in which ‘the children are come to the birth, and there is not strength to bring forth’ (Isaiah 37:3). The witnesses agree that the image of birth-pangs holds out hope as well as warning: the sorrows are real and will deepen, but they are the labour that ushers in a new order, so the people of God meet them with patience, not despair.
9:
‘Then shall they deliver you up… and ye shall be hated of all nations for my name’s sake.’ The witnesses agree Christ now turns from the nation’s woes to the disciples’ own persecutions; they divide on whether the verse looks to A.D. 70 or to the end.

The grammatical-historical commentators document the fulfilment. Barnes and Clarke trace it through Acts — Peter and John before the council (Acts 4:3), the stoning of Stephen (Acts 7:59), James killed by Herod (Acts 12:2), Paul before governors and kings, the Neronian persecution. Robertson notes the periphrastic future stressing ‘continuous’ hatred, and that the despised Name (Acts 5:41) is now ‘the most glorious… in the world.’ Ellicott cites Tacitus on Christians ‘hated for their crimes.’

The fathers mostly look beyond. Origen and Remigius refer it to the end, when the worship of idols being deserted, Christians will be blamed for wars and plagues, and persecution will be ‘everywhere’; Remigius offers the comfort that ‘more of the Gentiles should believe than of the Jews should perish.’ Chrysostom adds the third source of opposition — false brethren (2 Corinthians 11:26). On ‘the gospel preached in all the world,’ Augustine argues it is not yet wholly fulfilled, while the Gloss reconciles the views by distinguishing the fame of preaching (fulfilled before A.D. 70) from its fruit.

The Reformed Calvin notes the disciples are ‘hated and detested’ and subjected to the same punishments as criminals, bruised like wheat under the millstone, yet it is for the gospel’s sake. The shared note is that fidelity to the Name brings universal hatred, whether in the apostolic age or the last. Lightfoot ties the persecution to ‘the time is come that judgment must begin at the house of God’ (1 Peter 4:17), the Jews stirring up oppression everywhere as their ruin neared; Clarke notes that affliction is here personified, the disciples delivered ‘into affliction’s own hand.’ The witnesses agree that fidelity to Christ’s name brings a hatred that overleaps the bounds of race and kindred, whether in the apostolic age or, as the fathers hold, in the universal persecution that shall precede the end.
10:
‘Then shall many be offended, and shall betray one another, and shall hate one another.’ The witnesses agree this foretells apostasy and treachery among professed believers under the pressure of persecution.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the falling away. Barnes explains that many who professed love for Christ ‘shall then show that they had no real attachment,’ betraying others to secure their own safety (compare 1 John 2:19); Luke adds that they would be betrayed ‘by parents, and brethren, and kinsfolks.’ Clarke quotes Tacitus on the Neronian persecution, where the confession of a few led to the conviction of ‘a great multitude.’ Ellicott finds the fulfilment in the faction-fights of the Zealots (Acts 18:6 and the bitter Judaising hatred of Paul).

The Reformed Calvin treats this as ‘an exceedingly violent temptation’: Christ is ‘a stone of stumbling’ (1 Peter 2:8) on which many dash themselves, some falling away, some turning to rage, some growing profane. Gill reads the offended as those who professed Christianity ‘whilst things were tolerably quiet’ but stumbled at the cross when persecution came.

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the contrast: against the false prophets, ‘hold ye fast faith’; against abounding iniquity, ‘love’; against all, ‘hope, unto the end’ (v.13). Cyril of Jerusalem applies it sharply to his own day, when ‘bishops advance against bishops… even unto blood.’ The shared, dominant note is that persecution sifts the professing church, exposing those whose love was never real and turning former friends into betrayers — a trial the true disciple is to meet by holding fast faith, love, and hope to the end. Barnes explains that the apostates would betray others ‘to secure their own safety, by revealing the names, habitations, or places of concealment,’ the hatred of the gospel overcoming the tenderest natural bonds, so that men would be betrayed by parents and brethren and kinsfolk. The witnesses agree that persecution sifts the professing church, exposing those whose love was never real, and that the true disciple is to meet this trial by holding fast faith, love, and hope to the end. Cyril of Jerusalem applies the words sharply to his own age, when even bishops were found to advance against bishops, and bids the believer heed not the things now happening but the things written.
11:
‘Many false prophets shall rise, and shall deceive many.’ The witnesses, chiefly grammatical-historical with Calvin, distinguish these from the false Christs and document their rise.

The grammatical-historical commentators name them. Barnes refers, with Josephus, to the prophets who during the siege ‘declared that aid would be given… from heaven,’ suborned by the Zealots to keep the people from deserting. Clarke and Ellicott extend the reference to the heretical teachers of the apostolic age — Simon Magus, Hymeneus and Philetus (2 Timothy 2:17) — and to the ‘false teachers’ of 2 Peter 2:1 and the seducing spirits of 1 Timothy 4:1. Robertson notes Jesus had warned of them already in Matthew 7:15: ‘they are still coming.’

Gill lists the early heretics — Ebion, Cerinthus, Carpocrates, the Nicolaitans — who denied Christ’s deity or humanity under the Christian name. The Reformed Calvin marks the difference from v.5: there Christ spoke of impostors at the gospel’s commencement, but here he threatens that ‘in all ages false teachers will arise to corrupt sound doctrine’ (as 2 Peter 2:1 warns), and that they will be ‘crafty… to draw away sects after them.’

The shared, dominant note is that, alongside the false Messiahs, a steady succession of false prophets and teachers would deceive many — whether the fanatics who buoyed up Jerusalem to its ruin, or the heretics who corrupted the gospel in every age. Calvin’s warning stands for all: ‘the multitude of those who are going astray is like a violent tempest,’ and only those ‘firmly fixed on God’ will keep the course; the rise of error is no reason for dismay but the very trial of a faith founded on Christ. Barnes recalls from Josephus that the Zealots suborned prophets during the siege to keep the people from deserting with promises of heavenly aid, while Gill lists the early heretics who under the Christian name denied Christ’s deity or humanity. The witnesses agree that, distinct from the false Messiahs, a succession of false prophets and teachers would deceive many in every age, and that the tempest of error is no cause for dismay but the very trial of a faith firmly fixed on God.
12:
‘Because iniquity shall abound, the love of many shall wax cold.’ The witnesses agree this names the chilling of charity under abounding wickedness, a peril for the true as well as the false.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the terms. Barnes takes ‘iniquity’ to include the cruelty of persecutors, the betrayals, and the errors of false teachers, whose effect is that ‘the ardour of feeling of many Christians would be lessened.’ Ellicott renders it ‘lawlessness’ — no word better fitting Judea’s brigandage and massacre — and finds an instance of love waxing cold in the tendency to ‘forsake the assembling of themselves together’ (Hebrews 10:25). Robertson glosses the verb as ‘spiritual energy chilled by a malign wind,’ brotherly love giving way to ‘mutual hatred and suspicion.’ Lightfoot ties it to the ‘horrid apostasy’ foretold in 2 Thessalonians 2:3.

The Reformed Calvin probes the subtle danger to good men: seeing wickedness on every hand, even ‘well-regulated minds’ lose the desire to do good, reasoning that their kindnesses are wasted on the ungrateful; thus ‘charity, which ought to kindle the hearts of all… rather’ cools. The remedy he draws from 2 Thessalonians 3:13 — not to faint in well-doing.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem applies it to the feigned friendships of his own day. The shared, dominant note is that abounding iniquity has a freezing power even over genuine love, and that the disciple must guard against letting the malice of the times quench his charity. The cooling, the witnesses agree, is not merely the apostasy of hypocrites but a temptation pressing on the faithful, who are called to persevere in love when every example around them counsels its abandonment. Barnes takes ‘iniquity’ to embrace the cruelty of persecutors, the betrayals, and the errors of false teachers, whose combined effect would lessen the ardour even of real but feeble Christians; Ellicott points to the forsaking of the assembling together among the Hebrew Christians as one instance of love waxing cold. The witnesses agree that abounding wickedness has a freezing power even over genuine charity, so that the disciple must persevere in love when every example around him counsels its abandonment.
13:
‘But he that shall endure unto the end, the same shall be saved.’ The witnesses agree on perseverance as the condition of salvation, but divide on whether ‘the end’ means the end of life or the end of the Jewish war.

The grammatical-historical commentators give both senses. Barnes notes the word ‘end’ has been taken as the destruction of Jerusalem — he that perseveres ‘shall be safe,’ not a hair of the head perishing (Luke 21:18) — but, ‘with more probability,’ refers it to final salvation at the close of life; Luke adds, ‘In your patience possess ye your souls’ (Luke 21:19). Ellicott holds the words have ‘at once a higher and lower sense,’ the context pointing to deliverance from the doom of A.D. 70.

The Wesleyan Clarke presses the temporal reference with a striking fact: ‘not a single Christian perished in the destruction of Jerusalem,’ for when Cestius Gallus unexpectedly raised the siege, the believers escaped (he points forward to v.20). Gill combines the senses — enduring in faith, doctrine, and holiness ‘to the end of his life, or to the end of sorrows.’

The shared, dominant note is that salvation belongs to the persevering: he who holds fast faith, sound doctrine, and love through persecution and apostasy — whether to the end of the tribulation or the end of his days — is the one who is saved. The lower sense and the higher meet, for the same constancy that carried the Judean Christians safely out of the doomed city is the constancy that, persevering to life’s close, secures the soul; and so the verse is at once a historical promise of preservation and a standing call to endure, since only ‘he that endureth to the end’ lays hold of the salvation promised (Revelation 2:10). Gill combines the senses, the believer enduring in faith, doctrine, and holiness ‘to the end of his life, or to the end of sorrows’; and Wesley refers the verse to holding fast amid the false prophets and abounding iniquity already named. The witnesses agree that the lower and higher senses meet, for the constancy that carried the Christians safely out of the doomed city is the same that, held to life’s close, secures the soul.
14:
‘This gospel of the kingdom shall be preached in all the world… and then shall the end come.’ The witnesses agree the worldwide spread of the gospel is the sign preceding the end, but debate the scope of ‘all the world’ and which ‘end’ is meant.

The grammatical-historical commentators document the spread before A.D. 70. Barnes cites Paul that the gospel was ‘preached to every creature under heaven’ (Colossians 1:23), the faith of the Romans ‘spoken of throughout the whole world’ (Romans 1:8). Clarke, Ellicott and Robertson take ‘all the world’ (oikoumene) as the Roman empire (so Luke 2:1), and ‘the end’ as the destruction of Jerusalem; Ellicott marks ‘a witness unto all the nations’ as an implicit sanction of the Gentile mission, preparing for Matthew 28:19.

Lightfoot states the principle: Jerusalem was not to be destroyed ‘before the gospel was spread over all the world,’ that the world might have a testimony of Christ before vengeance fell on the nation that crucified him. The Reformed Calvin guards against over-literal application: Christ ‘does not absolutely refer to every portion of the world,’ but affirms that the gospel, thought to be confined to Judea, would reach the world’s farthest bounds before his last coming.

The Wesleyan Wesley agrees the gospel was preached ‘in general… before Jerusalem was destroyed,’ and commends Josephus’s history as ‘the best commentary on this chapter.’ Cyril of Jerusalem notes ‘nearly the whole world is now filled with the doctrine of Christ.’ The shared, dominant note is that the gospel’s diffusion — for a witness to all nations — must precede the end, leaving the world without excuse. Barnes marshals the proof that within thirty years of this prophecy churches were planted through Asia Minor, Greece, Crete, Italy and beyond, the faith of the Romans being ‘spoken of throughout the whole world.’ The witnesses agree that the gospel’s diffusion, for a witness to all the nations, must precede the end and leave the world without excuse; and that ‘a witness unto all the nations’ is an implicit sanction of the Gentile mission, preparing the way for the Great Commission.
15:
‘When ye shall see the abomination of desolation… stand in the holy place.’ This verse is the chapter’s great crux, and the witnesses divide widely over what the abomination is.

The grammatical-historical commentators, following Luke, identify it with the Roman army. Barnes, Clarke, Lightfoot and Robertson read it through Luke 21:20 — ‘when ye shall see Jerusalem compassed with armies’ — the legions being an ‘abomination’ for their idolatrous ensigns; Lightfoot renders Daniel’s ‘wing of abominations’ (Daniel 9:27) of the besieging army. Ellicott demurs: since the abomination stands in the holy place before the destruction, he locates it in the Zealots’ profanation of the temple, their murders and usurping priests (Josephus). Robertson notes ‘let the reader understand’ is Mark’s editorial insertion.

The fathers read it variously. Jerome offers the statue of Caesar set up by Pilate, or Hadrian’s equestrian statue, or Antichrist. Chrysostom and Pseudo-Chrysostom take it of the desolating army. Hilary, with the futurist reading, makes it Antichrist, who ‘claims to himself the honour of God’ in the holy place (compare 2 Thessalonians 2:4); Origen and Pseudo-Augustine allegorise it as ‘false word’ standing in the Scriptures.

The Reformed Calvin reads it of the temple’s profanation and end, confirmed from Daniel so the disciples might bear to be torn from the temple. Wesley takes the standards of the desolating legions; Cyril of Jerusalem, the Antichrist of 2 Thessalonians 2:4. The shared note is that a desolating profanation of the holy place — army, faction, or Antichrist — is the signal to flee. Lightfoot renders Daniel’s phrase ‘even by the wing of abominations making desolate,’ and bids the reader mark that when the army of the prince compasses Jerusalem, destruction is certain and no future victory is to be hoped for; Augustine appeals to Luke’s plain gloss, ‘when ye shall see Jerusalem compassed with armies, then know that her desolation is nigh’ (Luke 21:20). The witnesses thus divide between the desolating Roman army, the Zealots’ profanation of the sanctuary, and the Antichrist enthroned in the holy place, but all agree that a desolating profanation is the appointed signal for flight.
16:
‘Then let them which be in Judea flee into the mountains.’ The witnesses agree this is a literal command, remarkably obeyed, that spared the Judean church.

The grammatical-historical commentators record the fulfilment. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott and Robertson, citing Eusebius (Hist. Eccl. iii.5), relate that the Christians, warned, fled in A.D. 68 to Pella beyond the Jordan, so that ‘not a Christian’ perished in the city; Gill and Clarke note the providential withdrawal of Cestius Gallus, which gave the opening to escape. The ‘mountains’ are the hills of Gilead east of Jordan, traditional refuges (1 Samuel 22:1).

The Antiochene John Chrysostom presses the urgency: when the army appears, ‘thenceforth there is no hope of safety’; recalling how the Jews had often recovered under Sennacherib and the Maccabees, Christ forbids them all thought of such a change now. Remigius adds, from ecclesiastical history, that the believers crossed Jordan and took refuge at Pella under Agrippa.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson: the calamities will be so sudden that ‘time will scarcely be allowed for the most rapid flight,’ and Christ’s design is to wean his followers from the hope of ease and to fortify them. Wesley notes the ‘unexpected and impolitic’ retreat of Cestius Gallus as the very signal. Cyril of Jerusalem allows the godly to flee while the stout-hearted stand. The shared, dominant note is that the appearance of the abomination is the moment for instant flight: those who heeded the warning lived, and the providence that withdrew the besieging army gave them their opportunity — a vivid proof that Christ’s words were neither vague nor unfulfilled. Barnes notes that the mountains of Palestine, honeycombed with caves, were the immemorial refuge of the endangered (1 Samuel 22:1); and the witnesses agree that the appearance of the abomination was the moment for instant flight, the providence that unexpectedly withdrew the besieging army giving the believers their one opportunity to escape, so that Christ’s words proved neither vague nor unfulfilled. Chrysostom presses the urgency, that once the army appears there is no more hope of safety, and unlike the deliverances under Sennacherib or the Maccabees no such recovery is now to be looked for.
17:
‘Let him which is on the housetop not come down to take anything out of his house.’ The witnesses, all grammatical-historical, agree the verse commands instant, unencumbered flight, and explain it from the architecture of eastern houses.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the setting. Gill, Barnes and Clarke note the flat roofs of Jewish houses, used for retirement and prayer (compare Acts 10:9), reached by stairs on the outside. Clarke adds that in walled cities the roofs formed ‘continued terraces… from one end of the city to the other,’ so that a man might pass along the housetops to the gate and escape without entering his house. Robertson speaks of ‘the road of the roofs.’

The point all press is the haste. Barnes explains that the calamity would be ‘so sudden’ that to stop even to secure an article of clothing ‘would endanger his life.’ Ellicott and Wesley note that the danger — chiefly from the factions within the city — left no time for delay; men were to escape as though their life were ‘given them for a prey’ (Jeremiah 45:5), without a thought for their goods. Wesley adds the practical detail that ‘their stairs used to be on the outside of their houses.’

With so brief and harmonious a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: when the signal comes, escape must be immediate and total, the man on the roof fleeing by the rooftops to the city gate rather than descending to gather his possessions. The verse turns the very layout of an eastern town into an image of the speed the crisis would demand, and warns that any backward glance toward earthly goods, in such an hour, would be fatal — a lesson the next verses extend to the labourer in the field. The witnesses agree that the verse makes the suddenness of the crisis its whole point: deliverance would hang on instant obedience, and the man who paused even to secure his goods would lose his life, so that the very layout of the city — the rooftop road to the gate — became a parable of the haste the hour demanded.
18:
‘Neither let him which is in the field return back to take his clothes.’ The few witnesses, all grammatical-historical, continue the theme of urgent flight and note the detail of the labourer’s garment.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the picture. Barnes and Ellicott observe that the peasant worked in the short tunic, leaving the flowing outer garment (better, ‘his cloak’) at home; Robertson notes ‘the peasant worked in his tunic and left his mantle at home then as now.’ The command is that he must not even return for it, but flee at once from the field.

Clarke gives the reason from the course of events: ‘because when once the army of the Romans sits down before the city, there shall be no more any possibility of escape, as they shall never remove till Jerusalem be destroyed.’ Barnes adds the historical confirmation that the Christians, heeding these directions, fled to Pella and other places beyond Jordan, ‘so that there is not evidence that a single Christian perished in Jerusalem’ (Eusebius).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is necessarily short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: the worker in the field, like the man on the housetop (v.17), must flee instantly and unencumbered, not even pausing to recover the cloak he had laid aside for his labour. The verse drives home, in a second homely image, the speed the crisis would require — once the Roman army encircled the city escape would be impossible — and the obedience of the Judean Christians to just such warnings is offered as the proof that those who took Christ’s word at face value were preserved, while those who lingered for their possessions were lost. Robertson and Ellicott note the peasant’s short working tunic and the flowing cloak left at home, which he must not return for; the witnesses agree that the doubled image — housetop and field — drives home that once the siege closed in, only those already fled would be safe, and that the obedience of the Judean Christians to just such warnings was the means of their preservation.
19:
‘Woe unto them that are with child, and to them that give suck in those days.’ The few witnesses, all grammatical-historical, agree the tone is pity, not denunciation, and they point to the horrors of the siege.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the compassion. Gill is explicit that the words are ‘not expressive of sin, or punishment, but of pity and concern for their misery’: such women, by reason of their burdens, could not flee speedily and must endure the worst of the siege. Ellicott renders it ‘alas for them,’ the same tenderness shown to the daughters of Jerusalem (Luke 23:28-29).

The point all make is the peculiar hardship of flight for the encumbered. Barnes simply cross-refers to Luke’s woe (Luke 23:29). Clarke and Ellicott point to the darkest horrors recorded by Josephus — the famine in which mothers snatched food from their children, and the appalling case of Mary, who in the extremity of starvation killed and ate her own infant (Josephus, Wars vi.3).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the verse is a lament, not a curse, over those whom the flight would press most cruelly — expectant mothers and nursing women, least able to escape and most exposed to the famine and slaughter within the doomed city. Christ’s ‘woe’ is the voice of compassion foreseeing a grief beyond words, and the grim particulars preserved by Josephus, the commentators agree, show how literally and terribly the warning was fulfilled when the siege reduced the inhabitants to such extremities that the tenderest natural bonds were broken by hunger. Gill insists the words breathe pity, not punishment, since to bear children was always counted a blessing; the misery lies in the encumbered being least able to flee. The witnesses agree that Christ’s ‘woe’ is the voice of compassion foreseeing a grief beyond words, and that the horrors of the siege showed how literally the warning was fulfilled when hunger broke the tenderest natural bonds, the same tenderness sounding in his words to the daughters of Jerusalem.
20:
‘Pray ye that your flight be not in the winter, neither on the sabbath day.’ The witnesses agree this enjoins prayer about the circumstances of the flight, and note that Matthew alone, writing for Jews, records the sabbath clause.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain both clauses. Barnes, Clarke and Wesley note that winter’s cold, short days, and bad roads would make flight and lodging in the mountains a ‘double calamity’; Lightfoot quotes Rabbi Tanchum that God showed favour at the first temple’s fall by making it happen in summer, not winter. On the sabbath, Barnes and Ellicott explain that the Christians’ own scruples would limit them to a sabbath day’s journey (about a mile), too short to reach safety, and that gates would be shut and travel hindered by others’ observance (Exodus 16:29).

The point Barnes draws is that, though the destruction was certain and could not be prevented, it was right to ‘pray for a mitigation of the circumstances’ — as we may pray, knowing we must die, that ‘the pains of our dying may be neither long nor severe.’ Ellicott notes that what lay within their power was the subject of rule, what lay outside it the subject of prayer.

The Wesleyan Wesley records the answer to the prayer: ‘they did so; and their flight was in the spring.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ teaches his people to bring even the timing of their calamities to God in prayer, since he orders all events; and that the Judean believers, praying as bidden, were granted a flight neither in the deadly cold of winter nor on the day when their own and their neighbours’ sabbath-keeping would have trapped them — a further token that the providence which foretold their escape also tempered its hardships. Barnes draws the lesson that, though the calamity was certain and could not be prevented, it was right to pray it might come in as mild a form as God’s will allowed — as we may pray, knowing we must die, that the pains of dying be neither long nor severe. The witnesses agree that Christ teaches his people to bring even the timing of their trials to God, who orders all events, and that the believers’ flight, granted in the spring, was the answer to just such prayer.
21:
‘Then shall be great tribulation, such as was not since the beginning of the world.’ The witnesses agree this describes the unparalleled horror of Jerusalem’s fall, drawn from Daniel 12:1 and verified by Josephus.

The grammatical-historical commentators heap up the evidence. Barnes, following Luke (Luke 21:24), details the slaughter by the sword and the leading captive into all nations, quoting Josephus that ‘all the calamities… from the beginning of the world were but small in comparison.’ Clarke calls it a grief ‘almost impossible… to read… without weeping,’ the days of vengeance (Luke 21:22) in which the temple was burnt on the same day of the month as by the Babylonians. Ellicott grants that other sieges have seen equal physical wretchedness, but nothing parallel to the ‘alternations of fanatic hope and frenzied despair’ attending the breaking up of Israel.

The Reformed Calvin presses the cause: the tribulation answers the nation’s guilt, for they had not only despised but, ‘like persons mad or possessed,’ wreaked their cruelty on the Physician himself; God’s vengeance fell for their inveterate contempt of the gospel.

The Wesleyan Wesley raises the futurist question — ‘have not many things… a farther and much more extensive meaning than has been yet fulfilled?’ — and Cyril of Jerusalem refers the words to the tribulation under Antichrist, ‘confined… to a few days.’ The shared, dominant note is that the fall of Jerusalem was a tribulation without precedent, the just vengeance of God on a nation that had rejected its Messiah, though some hear in it an anticipation of a greater tribulation still to come. Barnes, following Luke, details the slaughter by the sword and the captivity into all nations, quoting Josephus that the Jews’ miseries surpassed all that had befallen any nation from the beginning of the world; Clarke calls these ‘the days of vengeance,’ noting the temple was burnt on the same day of the month as by the Babylonians. The witnesses agree that the fall of Jerusalem was a tribulation without precedent, the just vengeance of God on a nation that had rejected its Messiah.
22:
‘Except those days should be shortened, there should no flesh be saved: but for the elect’s sake those days shall be shortened.’ The witnesses agree the calamity would have exterminated the nation but for God’s mercy to his chosen.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain both clauses. Barnes and Clarke reckon, with Josephus, that eleven hundred thousand perished, and that had the war gone on ‘the whole nation… would… have been entirely extirpated’; the days were shortened by the Jews’ own factions and by Titus’s swift success. Robertson notes the historical means — the halting of the walls, the sudden arrival of Titus, the neglect to prepare for a long siege — and Titus’s own confession ‘that God was against the Jews.’ The ‘elect,’ they agree, are the Christians (Romans 8:33).

Lightfoot draws the tender point: God lengthened the time before the siege for the elect’s sake, and shortened it within the siege for their sake too, ‘lest… the elect should too far have partaken of their misery’ (compare 2 Peter 3:9).

The Reformed Calvin sets it under the doctrine of the remnant: but for God’s sparing, the Jews would have been ‘as Sodom’ (Isaiah 1:9), yet God remembers his covenant, and ‘a remnant only shall be saved’ (Romans 9:29). Wesley and Cyril of Jerusalem note the shortening — Cyril referring it to the three-and-a-half years of Antichrist (Daniel 7:25). The shared, dominant note is that the days of vengeance were mercifully limited for the sake of God’s elect, a striking proof both of the severity of his judgment and of his faithful care for his own. Barnes and Clarke reckon, with Josephus, that some eleven hundred thousand perished, and that had the war continued the whole nation would have been extirpated; the days were shortened by the Jews’ own factions and by Titus’s swift and providential success. The witnesses agree that the days of vengeance were mercifully limited for the sake of God’s elect, a striking proof both of the severity of his judgment and of his faithful care for his own.
23:
‘Then if any man shall say unto you, Lo, here is Christ, or there; believe it not.’ The witnesses agree this renews the warning against false Christs, and most now read it of the period after Jerusalem — the second coming and Antichrist.

The fathers turn here to the last things. Chrysostom marks that ‘then’ leaps over ‘the whole interval… between the taking of Jerusalem and… the consummation’: Christ’s second coming will not be hidden in a corner, as Bethlehem, but open, so it is folly to seek him in ‘places little and obscure.’ Hilary says false prophets will claim Christ is ‘in the desert’ or ‘in the secret chambers’ to enthral men to Antichrist. Augustine warns that ‘wicked men shall do some miracles which the saints cannot,’ yet are not therefore higher with God. Gregory and Raban explain that ‘the very elect’ cannot truly be deceived — ‘it is not possible.’

The grammatical-historical commentators keep the nearer reference too. Barnes and Clarke note that in the city’s extremity the Jews, expecting a Messiah to deliver them, would catch at any impostor (compare Deuteronomy 13:1-3); Robertson sees the false prophets and false Christs offering ‘a way out of these troubles.’

The Reformed Calvin presses the danger: in desperate straits men are deceived ‘under the disguise of the name of God,’ and false christs come armed with signs ‘fitted to confound weak minds.’ Cyril of Jerusalem, citing 2 Thessalonians 2:3, makes the hatred of brethren the prelude to Antichrist. The shared, dominant note is that no claim of a hidden, local Christ is to be believed, for his true coming will be unmistakable and universal. Chrysostom marks that the true coming will be open, not hidden in a corner as at Bethlehem, so that it is folly to seek Christ in obscure places; Augustine warns that the wicked may work wonders the saints cannot, yet stand no higher with God, since they seek their own glory and not his. The witnesses agree that no claim of a hidden, local Christ is to be believed, for his real coming will be unmistakable and universal, and that the elect, though assailed, cannot finally be deceived.
24:
‘There shall arise false Christs and false prophets, and shall shew great signs and wonders; insomuch that, if it were possible, they shall deceive the very elect.’ The witnesses agree on the danger of lying miracles and on the security of the elect.

The grammatical-historical commentators document the impostors. Gill and Barnes name Bar-Cochba, hailed as Messiah by Akiba, and the magicians and sorcerers Josephus describes leading the people into the desert with promises of miracles. Ellicott points to Simon Magus (Acts 8:9-11) and Elymas (Acts 13:6), and to the ‘signs and lying wonders’ of the Wicked One (2 Thessalonians 2:9). Robertson stresses that ‘mere signs and wonders do not of themselves prove the power of God’; these charlatans are so skilful that they would deceive the elect ‘if possible — the implication is that it is not possible.’ Lightfoot adds the Jews’ addiction to dreams and curious arts.

The Reformed Calvin draws the comfort: this is added ‘to excite alarm, that believers may be more careful,’ yet they are safe ‘under the defense and protection of God’; it is ‘not possible for them to fall away from salvation, to whom the Son of God is a faithful guardian’ (John 10:28). Wesley agrees: ‘it is not possible that God should suffer the body of Christians to be thus deceived.’

The shared, dominant note is that the great signs of false christs are no proof of divine power, and that the elect, though warned to vigilance, are kept secure in Christ’s hand. The ‘if it were possible’ is read by the witnesses as an assurance, not a threat: the deceptions will be so plausible that none but the elect could withstand them, yet the very point is that they cannot finally prevail against those whom the Son of God keeps; the warning sharpens watchfulness while the promise undergirds confidence. Lightfoot adds the Jews’ notorious addiction to dreams and curious arts, which made them the readier prey to such pretenders; and the witnesses agree that lying signs are no proof of divine power, so that the warning sharpens watchfulness while the promise of the elect’s security undergirds an unshaken confidence.
25:
‘Behold, I have told you before.’ The few witnesses agree this brief word underlines the value of forewarning and the inexcusability of being deceived.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the purpose. Gill explains that Christ had warned them ‘before these things came to pass,’ so that they would be ‘inexcusable if they were not upon their guard,’ and that the fulfilment would prove him the true Messiah, ‘showing him to be omniscient’ and establishing their faith. Barnes, with Mark’s ‘take ye heed’ (Mark 13:23), reads it as given ‘that they might be on their guard, and be prepared.’ Clarke glosses it simply: ‘I have forewarned you.’

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: those ‘who are dismayed by the stumbling-blocks which Christ predicted are altogether inexcusable,’ since the will of God, once made known, is our rule; and he points to the promise that God ‘is faithful, and will not suffer us to be tempted beyond what we are able to bear’ (1 Corinthians 10:13), so that ‘we shall never be in want of strength to resist,’ provided our weakness be not nourished by indifference.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the very fact of Christ’s forewarning is itself a mercy and a test. It is a mercy, because to be told beforehand of the deceivers is to be armed against them; it is a test, because those who are nonetheless dismayed or led astray have no excuse, the will of God having been plainly declared. And it is, finally, an evidence: when the predicted things came to pass, the disciples would have in Christ’s foreknowledge a confirmation of his Messiahship and a steadying of their faith against every false christ and lying wonder. Gill adds that the warning, given before the events, would when fulfilled furnish ‘a considerable argument’ for Christ as the true Messiah, showing him omniscient; and Barnes, with Mark’s ‘take ye heed,’ reads it as given that they might be on their guard and prepared. The witnesses agree that the forewarning is at once a mercy that arms the believer, a test that leaves the deceived inexcusable, and an evidence that steadies faith when the predicted things come to pass.
26:
‘If they shall say unto you, Behold, he is in the desert… or in the secret chambers; believe it not.’ The witnesses agree this specifies the false reports about Messiah’s whereabouts, which the believer is to disregard.

The grammatical-historical commentators document the pattern. Barnes notes the Jews expected the Messiah to appear ‘suddenly, from some unexpected quarter,’ so impostors led the people into the deserts; Clarke, citing Josephus and Acts 21:38, lists the Egyptian who led four thousand into the desert, and others — Jonathan the weaver, and the false prophet who drew the people into the temple chambers. Robertson names Simon son of Gioras (the wilderness) and John of Giscala (the inner chambers). Ellicott explains that the pretenders ‘shun the publicity which would test their claims,’ whispering that Christ is concealed and will appear at the last moment.

The Reformed Calvin sets it against the true coming: the contrast is between ‘a state of concealment’ and the sudden, far-reaching extension of Christ’s kingdom; the false christs ‘drew along with them… large bodies of men… into the recesses of the’ desert, but the believer is to walk in the light while he has it (John 12:35).

The shared, dominant note is that any report locating Christ in some hidden or remote place — the desert, the secret chamber — is to be rejected out of hand, for it contradicts the very manner of his coming. The impostors trade on concealment precisely because it evades the test of open scrutiny; but the true Christ, as the following verse will declare, comes like the lightning, visible from east to west, so that no one need go out to seek him in a corner. The believer’s safeguard is to know beforehand that his Lord’s return will be universal and unmistakable, leaving no room for the whispered claims of those who would point him to the wilderness or the inner room. Clarke marvels that Christ foretold not only the impostors but the very manner of their conduct — some in the desert, some in the secret chambers — each minutely verified by Josephus; and the witnesses agree that the true Christ comes like the lightning, visible from east to west, leaving no room for the whispered claims of those who would point him to the wilderness or the inner room.
27:
"For as the lightning cometh out of the east, and shineth even unto the west; so shall also the coming of the Son of man be." The witnesses agree the point of the simile is manner, not direction — a coming sudden, public, and unmistakable — but they divide over which "coming" is meant, opening the great debate that runs through the chapter. The grammatical-historical Barnes states the sense: "He does not mean to affirm that the Son of man will come from the east, but that he will come in a rapid and unexpected manner, like the lightning"; it was "useless to be looking in the desert" for him. He frames the recurring question precisely: the destruction of Jerusalem and the final judgment "in many respects greatly resemble each other," and "the same words often include both events." Robertson notes the verb means "visible in contrast to the invisibility of the false Messiahs" (Revelation 1:7), "like a flash of lightning."

The preterist reading is pressed by Lightfoot and Clarke. Lightfoot lays down two keys for the whole chapter: that "the destruction of Jerusalem is very frequently expressed in Scripture as if it were the destruction of the whole world" (Deuteronomy 32:22; Isaiah 65:17), and that Christ's "taking vengeance of that exceeding wicked nation is called Christ's coming in glory... in the clouds" (Daniel 7). Clarke notes the lightning "out of the east" traces "the very march of the Roman army," which "entered into Judea on the East, and carried on their conquest westward."

Ellicott holds the two together: "we are on the dim border-land of the primary and the ultimate fulfilments." The disciples "had connected the destruction of Jerusalem with the coming of their Lord, and the two are connected even in His own words"; in whatever way he came, "the advent would be sudden and unlooked-for as the lightning-flash," and "the crises of the world's history... are always unexpected by the great mass of mankind." Wesley keeps it simple: the coming "will be as quick as lightning, so that there will not be time for any such previous warning." The witnesses thus set the chapter's interpretive tension at once, agreeing only that the coming, whichever is in view, will be sudden and self-evident.
28:
"For wheresoever the carcase is, there will the eagles be gathered together." The witnesses sharply divide on this proverb, and Ellicott helpfully names the two main readings he rejects. The dominant grammatical-historical and preterist line takes the carcass as doomed Jerusalem and the eagles as the Roman legions. Lightfoot: "when God shall cast off the city and people, grown ripe for destruction, like a carcase thrown out, the Roman soldiers, like eagles, shall straight fly to it with their eagles (ensigns) to tear and devour it." Clarke notes the Roman ensigns bore the eagle, and "the Roman fury pursued these wretched men wheresoever they were found." Gill applies it to "the people of the Jews... in their fallen, deplorable, miserable, and lifeless state." Wesley: "Expect not any deliverer of the Jewish nation; for it is devoted to destruction... a dead carcass, which the Roman eagles will soon devour."

Barnes and Robertson read it as a proverb of universal application: the bird (perhaps "the griffon vulture") finds the carcass as surely as "the providence of God will direct" the instruments of chastisement to the wicked (Job 39:30; Proverbs 30:17). Ellicott generalizes it further: "wherever a church or nation is decaying and putrescent, there to the end of time will God's ministers of vengeance... assuredly be found" (Isaiah 46:11; Ezekiel 39:4), fulfilled "on the largest scale at the end of all things."

Ellicott firmly rejects the patristic allegory, "the strange fantastic imagination of many of the Fathers that the carcass is Christ Himself, as crucified and slain, and that the eagles are His true saints." The Reformed Calvin, however, defends a version of it: "in Christ himself is the sacred bond of union, by which [the children of God] must be kept united," arguing "from the less to the greater, that if birds have so great sagacity as to flock... to a single carcass, it would be disgraceful in believers not to assemble to the Author of life." Yet Calvin too notes the Greek means a dead body, and resists allegorizing the eagles into "acute and sagacious men." The witnesses agree the proverb teaches an inevitable convergence — most reading it of judgment on a spiritually dead Jerusalem, a few of the gathering of believers to Christ.
29:
"Immediately after the tribulation of those days shall the sun be darkened... and the powers of the heavens shall be shaken." This is the storm-centre of the chapter's debate, and the witnesses divide over both the imagery and the timing-word "immediately." The preterist voices read it of Jerusalem's fall in symbolic language. Lightfoot: "the Jewish heaven shall perish... The sun is the religion of the church; the moon is the government of the state; and the stars are the judges and doctors of both" (Isaiah 13:10; Ezekiel 32:7). Gill insists the word "immediately" forbids referring it to a far-off judgment: "all the following things were to be fulfilled before that present generation." Clarke marshals the prophetic idiom — Babylon, Egypt, the Jews under Antiochus all described by darkened sun and falling stars — concluding "this general mode of describing these judgments leaves no room to doubt the propriety of its application."

The futurist and double-reference voices read it of the second Advent. Ellicott: "from this point onwards the prophecy takes a wider range, and passes beyond... to the final coming of the Son of Man." He meets the delay of "more than eighteen centuries" with the analogy of "two snow-crowned mountains apparently in close proximity," the vast tract between unseen — and with the kenotic note that "of that day knew no man, not even the Son" (v.36). Robertson: "Literalism is not appropriate in this apocalyptic eschatology." The Latin and Alexandrian fathers read it of the last day: Chrysostom takes the tribulation as "the times of Antichrist," and finds "the sign of the cross" appearing "far brighter than the rays of the sun"; Jerome holds the lights are not annihilated but dimmed "by comparison with real light"; Augustine reads sun, moon, and stars allegorically as the Church under persecution.

The Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry take the long view. Calvin reads the whole as "a general recapitulation of all the evils," teaching that Christ's glory "will not be immediately, but after that the Church shall have passed through the whole course of its afflictions"; the prophets' magnificent language covers "the whole course" of redemption "till it should arrive at perfection." Henry: "It is usual for prophets to speak of things as near and just at hand, to express the greatness and certainty of them." Wesley invokes "with whom a thousand years are as one day." The witnesses agree only that the language is apocalyptic; they part over whether its primary referent is A.D. 70 or the end of the world.
30:
"Then shall appear the sign of the Son of man in heaven... and they shall see the Son of man coming in the clouds of heaven with power and great glory." The witnesses debate what the "sign" is and, again, which coming is meant. On the sign, several patristic and later voices identify it as the cross. Origen and Cyril of Jerusalem hold "Christ's own true sign is the Cross; a sign of a luminous Cross shall go before the King," so that the Jews "may mourn tribe by tribe." But Ellicott rejects such conjectures — "the cross appearing in the sky... the lightning flash" — and answers from Daniel 7:13: "The sign of the Son of Man is none other than the presence of the Son of Man Himself, coming in the clouds of heaven." Robertson agrees, taking it as "the genitive of apposition," Christ himself being the sign the Jews had so often demanded (Matthew 12:38; 16:1).

The preterist Lightfoot reads the "sign" as the vengeance itself: "the Son of man [shall] give a proof of himself... not in any visible figure, but in vengeance and judgment so visible, that all the tribes of the earth shall be forced to acknowledge him the avenger." Gill agrees the sign is "the son of man himself," and Clarke that the destruction of Jerusalem was "such a remarkable instance of Divine vengeance... that all the Jewish tribes shall mourn." Barnes holds both: "at the destruction of Jerusalem, the sign... was found in the fulfillment of these predictions; at the end of the world, the sign... will be his personal approach with the glory of his Father."

On "all the tribes of the earth mourn," the witnesses connect it to Zechariah 12:10 and Revelation 1:7. Ellicott: the mourning includes "all who in any age crucify the Son of God afresh" (Hebrews 6:6). The Reformed Calvin reads the verse as marking "the difference between the present condition of his kingdom and its future glory": now his majesty is hidden, but at his last coming "the heavenly power... will serve as a standard displayed to compel the whole world to look at him," and the mockers shall "look on him whom they pierced." Henry: "At his first coming, he was set for a sign that should be spoken against, but at his second coming, a sign that should be admired." Wesley ventures the cross "perhaps." The witnesses agree the coming is glorious and visible; they differ on whether its first reference is the avenging of A.D. 70 or the final Advent.
31:
"And he shall send his angels with a great sound of a trumpet, and they shall gather together his elect from the four winds." The witnesses split cleanly here between the preterist and futurist readings, with the meaning of "angels" the hinge. The preterist voices take the angels as gospel messengers gathering the Gentile elect after Jerusalem's fall. Gill: "not the angels, i.e. ministering spirits, but men angels, or messengers, the ministers and preachers of the Gospel, whom Christ would call... to preach his Gospel, and plant churches" among the Gentiles. Lightfoot: "when Jerusalem shall be reduced to ashes... then shall the Son of man send his ministers with the trumpet of the gospel." Clarke reads "the elect" as "the Gentiles, who were now chosen in place of the rebellious Jews," and appends Josephus's vast catalogue of the slain and captive to show the prophecy's literal fulfilment.

The futurist voices read it of the final gathering at the resurrection. Chrysostom: "the sound of the trump refers to the resurrection," when "the Angels shall gather together those that have risen." Barnes allows both but says "the language seems to refer rather to the end of the world... the gathering of his elect at the day of judgment" (1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16). Ellicott connects it to the parables of the Tares and the Net (Matthew 13:41, 49) and to the sealing of the elect in Revelation 7:2, holding "the elect are those who are living on the earth at the time of the second Advent." Robertson is decisive: "the coming of the son of man is not to be identified with the judgment of Jerusalem but rather forms its preternatural background" (Bruce).

The Alexandrian Origen reads it spiritually and broadly: the angels gather not only the living but "all from the foundation of the world, who like Abraham have seen the day of Christ" (John 8:56), "from one end of heaven to the other." The Reformed Calvin makes it pastoral comfort: "though the Church be now tormented by the malice of men, or even broken by the violence of the billows... we ought always to cherish confident hope, because it will not be by human means, but by heavenly power... that the Lord will gather his Church." The witnesses agree the verse promises an ingathering of the elect; they differ on whether it is the gospel's spread after A.D. 70 or the resurrection-harvest at the end.
32:
"Now learn a parable of the fig tree; When his branch is yet tender, and putteth forth leaves, ye know that summer is nigh." The witnesses agree the parable teaches that the foregoing signs herald the nearness of what Christ foretold, and several note it was likely drawn from a fig tree before them on Olivet (Ellicott, Barnes). The Antiochene Chrysostom reads it as showing "the interval of time shall not be great," and the "spiritual summer and peace that the just shall enjoy after their winter, while sinners shall have a winter after summer." The Latin and Alexandrian fathers add allegory: Origen sees the world's "vital energies dormant within them as in a season of winter" until "Christ's Spirit breathing upon them"; Augustine reads the fig tree as "the human race"; Hilary, as "the Synagogue," its tender branch "Antichrist."

The puritan Henry draws the practical use and introduces Grotius's crucial distinction: "there is a manifest distinction made between the tauta (v.34) and the ekeine (v.36), these things and that day and hour." The "these things" — wars, persecutions, "especially the ruin of the Jewish nation" — would befall "this generation"; but "that day and hour which will put a period to time, that knoweth no man." So Henry warns against "confounding these two, as they did who... inferred that the day of Christ was at hand" (2 Thessalonians 2:2). Of the signs he says, "when God begins to fulfil prophecies, he will make an end."

The grammatical-historical voices keep it concrete: Barnes and Clarke read the budding fig as "an infallible proof of the approaching ruin of the Jewish state," while Ellicott takes it more generally — "as surely as the fresh green foliage of the fig tree is a sign of summer, so shall the signs of which He speaks portend the coming of the Son of Man." The Reformed Calvin offers a subtler reading: as the tree in spring loses its winter toughness and is "even cleft asunder to open up passage for fresh twigs," so "the afflictions by which the Church is softened do not impair its vigor" but precede "immortal glory" (2 Corinthians 4:16). Wesley applies Grotius's distinction directly: Christ answers the question of Jerusalem's time at v.34 and of the end of the world at v.36. The witnesses agree the parable bids us read the signs of the times — though they differ, as throughout, on which "summer" is chiefly in view.
33:
"So likewise ye, when ye shall see all these things, know that it is near, even at the doors." The witnesses apply the fig-tree parable, and the comment is brief and largely grammatical. The Antiochene Chrysostom reads it as confirming the nearness: as the fig's budding is "of necessity" followed by summer, so Christ's words "indicating that the interval was not great" will "in quick succession" be fulfilled — "a spiritual summer, and a calm... for the righteous; but to the sinners the contrary, winter after summer." Gill takes "all these things" of "the signs of the destruction of the temple and city, and the destruction itself."

Barnes draws together the Synoptic parallels: "when you see what I have predicted, the signs around Jerusalem, then know that its destruction is at hand"; Luke says "your redemption draweth nigh" and "the kingdom of God is nigh at hand" (Luke 21:28, 31) — "your deliverance from the dangers that threaten the city approaches... or your everlasting redemption from sin and death will come at the day of judgment." He notes the marginal reading "He is near" rather than "it is near" (James 5:9).

Ellicott fastens on the emphatic pronoun: "Ye whom I have chosen, who are therefore among the elect... The words are spoken to the four Apostles as the representatives of the whole body of believers who should be living — first, at the destruction of Jerusalem, and afterwards at the end of the world. Of the four, St. John alone, so far as we know, survived the destruction of Jerusalem." He too prefers the reading "that He is near, in accordance with James 5:9."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which simply completes the parable of v.32. The witnesses agree on its plain force: the signs Christ has named are not idle portents but harbingers, and when they appear the watchful are to recognize that the predicted event stands "even at the doors." The double-reference reading runs through the comment as elsewhere — the nearness is, primarily, the nearness of Jerusalem's fall to the apostles' generation, and, by extension, the nearness of the Lord's final coming to every generation that reads the signs of its own times. The verse, like the parable it applies, calls not for date-setting but for discernment and readiness.
34:
"Verily I say unto you, This generation shall not pass, till all these things be fulfilled." This crux verse forces the chapter's debate to a head, and the witnesses divide over the meaning of "this generation." The preterist and grammatical-historical majority takes it of the contemporaries then living. Lightfoot: "hence it appears plain enough that the foregoing verses are not to be understood of the last judgment, but... of the destruction of Jerusalem. There were some among the disciples (particularly John) who lived to see these things." Ellicott is emphatic: "the natural meaning of the words is, beyond question, that which takes generation in the ordinary sense... for those who are living at any given period," and "for this meaning [as 'race'] there is not the shadow of authority." Barnes: "the destruction of Jerusalem took place about forty years after this was spoken"; the events "would be such as to make appropriate the words of the prediction, were there nothing beyond," while leaving room for "a fulness of signification" reaching to the judgment. Robertson and Wesley concur, Wesley noting "the city and temple were destroyed thirty-nine or forty years after."

A minority, troubled by the apparent difficulty, takes "this generation" as "this race" — the Jewish people. Clarke: "this race; i.e. the Jews shall not cease from being a distinct people, till all the counsels of God relative to them and the Gentiles be fulfilled" (Romans 11:25). The Antiochene Chrysostom reads it of "the generation of the faithful," teaching that "Jerusalem shall perish... but that no trial shall overthrow the generation of the faithful." The puritan Henry follows Grotius, applying "these things" of v.34 to the ruin of the Jewish nation within that generation, and reserving "that day and hour" (v.36) for the final judgment.

The Reformed Calvin holds firmly to the contemporary sense, but extends the warning: Christ "does not by any means exempt future ages from the same kind of sufferings," yet "before the close of a single generation believers would feel... the truth of his prediction; for within fifty years the city was destroyed and the temple was razed." Clarke's long note documents the fulfilment with Josephus, Titus's triumphal arch, and the "Judaea Capta" medal. The witnesses thus largely agree that "this generation" means the apostles' contemporaries and that the destruction of Jerusalem fulfilled the word — the chief dissent being those who soften "generation" to "race" to ease the difficulty Ellicott says it cannot remove.
35:
"Heaven and earth shall pass away, but my words shall not pass away." The witnesses receive this as Christ's solemn seal upon his prophecy, claiming for his own words the permanence of God's. Barnes states it plainly: "You may sooner expect to see the heaven and earth pass away, and return to nothing, than my words to fail" (Psalms 102:26; Isaiah 51:6). Gill weighs whether the heavens and earth shall "pass away" in substance or only in quality — favouring renewal over annihilation — but takes the main force as comparative: "the heavens and the earth... than which nothing can be more firm and strong... shall sooner pass away, than anything asserted and predicted by Christ shall."

Ellicott hears in it the accent of divine authority: "The tone is that of One who speaks with supreme authority, foreseeing... the ultimate victory, not of truth only in the abstract, but of His own word as the truth." The parallel with Psalms 102:26 and Isaiah 40:8 "gives them their full significance. The Son of Man claims for His own words the eternity which belongs to the words of Jehovah" (1 Peter 1:24-25). And he points to the verifying history: "Amid all its changes and confusions... the words of Christ have not passed away, but retain their pre-eminence as the last and fullest revelation of the Father."

The Reformed Calvin reads it as designed "to raise the minds of his followers above the contemplation of the world." Rather than fixing on the last day or on annihilation, Calvin takes it of "the continual changes which we see in the world": Christ "affirms that we ought not to judge of his sayings by the changeful character of the world, which resembles the billows of the sea." Hence the comfort: "our salvation, because it is founded on the promises of Christ, does not fluctuate according to the various agitations of the world, but remains unshaken, provided only that our faith rises above and ascends to Christ himself." The puritan Henry (under v.32) had said the same: "We may build with more assurance upon the word of Christ than we can upon the pillars of heaven... when they shall be made to totter, and shall be no more, the word of Christ shall remain."

The witnesses agree without dissent: amid the apocalyptic upheavals just described, and amid the world's ceaseless flux, the one fixed and imperishable thing is the word of Christ. Whatever shakes — sun, moon, stars, the very frame of heaven and earth — his sayings stand, and on them faith may rest as on a foundation surer than creation itself.
36:
"But of that day and hour knoweth no man, no, not the angels of heaven, but my Father only." The witnesses agree this marks the unknowable final day, and they grapple with Mark's harder reading "neither the Son" (Mark 13:32) and its Christological weight. The grammatical-historical voices stress the deliberate hiddenness. Barnes: "the precise time would not be foretold," only the general signs, "as the budding of the fig-tree." Robertson holds "in this verse Jesus has in mind the time of his second coming," distinct from v.34's events: "no one but the Father knows the day or the hour"; and he resists McNeile's charge that Jesus expected the End within his contemporaries' lifetime — "that after his explicit denial that he knew anything of the kind!"

On "neither the Son," the Latin and Alexandrian fathers labour to defend Christ's omniscience against the Arians.
