Jerome reasons that the Word "made all times," so cannot be ignorant of "a part," and explains the saying by Acts 1:7 — Christ "shows that Himself knows, but that it was not expedient for the Apostles to know." Augustine: "knows not" means "makes others not to know," as God said to Abraham "Now I know that thou fearest God" (Genesis 22:12). Origen: "so long as the Church which is Christ's body knows not that day, so long the Son Himself is said not to know." Hilary and Raban add their solutions; Jerome notes the clause is absent from the best Greek copies.

The Reformed Calvin and grammatical Ellicott accept a real human limitation through the kenosis. Ellicott: "It is obviously doing violence to the plain meaning of the words to dilute them into the statement that the Son of Man did not communicate the knowledge which He possessed as the Son of God"; the eternal Word, "in becoming flesh, emptied Himself" (Philippians 2:7) and "took upon Him the limitations necessarily incidental to man's nature." Calvin: "in Christ the two natures were united... in such a manner that each retained its own properties," the Divine nature "in a state of repose" when the human acted; so Christ "as man, did not know the last day," which "does not any more derogate from his Divine nature than to have been mortal." The puritan Henry: "That day and hour are a great secret... one of those secret things which belong to the Lord our God." The witnesses agree the day is hidden by design — to keep us watchful (v.42) — while dividing on whether the Son's "ignorance" is real (in his humanity) or a manner of speaking.
37:
"But as the days of Noe were, so shall also the coming of the Son of man be." The witnesses read Noah's flood as the type of a sudden judgment falling on a careless, feasting world. The Antiochene Chrysostom: Christ "shall come sudden and unlooked for, and while men are taking their pleasure," as Paul says, "When they shall say, Peace and safety, then sudden destruction cometh" (1 Thessalonians 5:3); the wicked "wantoned as though no evil should ever come." Barnes states the two points of likeness: the coming will be "sudden and unexpected, the precise time not being made known," and "the world would be found" absorbed in ordinary affairs.

The preterist Lightfoot notes that Peter pairs "the ruin of the old world and the ruin of Jerusalem" (1 Peter 3:19-21; 2 Peter 3:6-7), the men of Noah's day "buried in security" and so "surprised by the flood," as the Jews would be. Ellicott observes the same Petrine echo and conjectures that Mark and Luke, "writing for the Gentile Christians," omitted the Noah reference as "not so familiar." Clarke and Robertson stress the contrast between ample warning and "utter unpreparedness": "In Noah's day there was plenty of warning, but utter unpreparedness."

The Reformed Calvin draws the precise lesson, guarding against misreading the eating and drinking as the sin: "in itself it would not have been improper to make provision for their wants, if they had not with gross stupidity opposed the judgment of God." The fault was "obstinacy" and "stupid indifference" — promising themselves "that the condition in which they then were would remain unchanged," they "rushed, with closed eyes, to unbridled iniquity, as if there had been no Judge in heaven." So "the last age of the world will be in a state of stupid indifference, so that men will think of nothing but the present life." Wesley simply cross-references Luke 17:26.

The witnesses agree the warning is against carnal security. The point is not that ordinary life is sinful, but that to be wholly absorbed in it, deaf to the warnings of God and heedless of the coming reckoning, is to be swept away unprepared — as the antediluvians were by the flood, and as the careless will be at the coming of the Son of Man. The example, like the chapter as a whole, has a double edge: it warned the apostles' generation of the deluge of Roman vengeance about to engulf a secure Jerusalem, and it warns every generation of the sudden, final advent that will overtake a world "eating and drinking" as if it would never end. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here belongs to another discourse, not this verse.)
38:
"For as in the days that were before the flood they were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage, until the day that Noe entered into the ark." The brief verse draws only short comment, the witnesses being Gill, Barnes, and Robertson. Gill notes the reference is "not all the days before the flood, from the creation of the world; but those immediately preceding it, a century or two before it" — the generation of Noah's own day, warned across the long years of the ark's building.

Barnes states the point with care, guarding against turning ordinary life into sin: "The things mentioned here denote attention to the affairs of this life, rather than to what was coming on them. It does not mean that these things were wrong, but only that such was their actual employment, and that they were regardless of what was coming upon them." Eating, drinking, marrying — these are the common and lawful business of human existence; the fault lay not in doing them but in doing nothing else, in being so wholly engrossed in the present that the warning of judgment made no impression. Robertson adds a lexical touch on "were eating" (a periphrastic imperfect), noting the verb "means to chew raw vegetables or fruits like nuts or almonds" — a small detail of the ordinary, unhurried life that went on undisturbed to the very edge of catastrophe.

The thin comment matches the verse, which simply itemizes the careless normalcy of the antediluvian world that v.37 introduced. The witnesses agree on its force: the picture is not of gross debauchery but of a settled, comfortable absorption in daily affairs, continued "until the day that Noe entered into the ark" — that is, up to the last possible moment, with no thought of the deluge poised to fall. The four activities named — two of appetite, two of family life — span the whole round of human concern, and their very innocence sharpens the warning. It is possible to be lost not by spectacular wickedness but by simple inattention, by letting the legitimate business of living crowd out all regard for the God who will come. The next verse drives the point home: they "knew not until the flood came" — and so, Christ says, shall the coming of the Son of Man be.
39:
"And knew not until the flood came, and took them all away; so shall also the coming of the Son of man be." The witnesses fasten on the willful ignorance of Noah's generation as the type of the careless world Christ will overtake. Gill: "they did not advert or give heed to what Noah said... they slighted and despised his warnings; they did not believe... and therefore God gave them up to judicial blindness and hardness of heart." Barnes: "they knew not the exact time, until it came upon them. So shall it be when the Son of man shall come. They shall not know the precise time until he comes, and then they shall be found engaged in the ordinary business of life unconcerned." Clarke: "they considered not — did not lay Noah's warning to heart, till it was too late to profit by it."

Ellicott draws the conclusion that, for him, settles the chapter's interpretation: "The words justify the interpretation given above of Matthew 24:29-30. If the signs of the Advent were to be phenomena visible to the eye of sense, there could not be this reckless apathy of nescience. If they are to be tokens, signs of the times, which can be discerned only by the illumined insight of the faithful, the hardened unbelief on the one side, and the expectant watchfulness on the other, are the natural result." The very unawareness of the world proves the signs are spiritual, read only by faith.

The Reformed Calvin traces the ignorance to unbelief: "unbelief had blinded their minds; as, on the other hand, Noah, beheld at a distance, by the eyes of faith, the vengeance of God which was still concealed" (Hebrews 11:7). He notes that warning was not wholly withheld — Noah, "in building the ark for more than a hundred years, presented a warning of the approaching calamity" — but only one man "was specially warned... and raised up to cherish the hope of salvation." So now, "though the report of the last judgment is now widely circulated," only "a few persons who have been taught by God" truly perceive it; like Noah's small saved company in the ark, "to this small number our minds must be directed, if we desire to escape in safety" (1 Peter 3:20-21).

The witnesses agree: the deadly thing was not the absence of warning but the refusal to heed it. The flood "took them all away" not because they were uninformed but because they were unbelieving — and so the coming of the Son of Man will find a world that has heard the warning and dismissed it, swept off in the midst of its unconcern.
40:
"Then shall two be in the field; the one shall be taken, and the other left." The witnesses read this as the great separation that the coming will effect, sudden and discriminating, dividing those outwardly alike. The preterist voices apply it to the calamity of A.D. 70. Clarke: "so general should these calamities be, that no two persons, wheresoever found... should be both able to effect their escape; captivity and the sword should have a complete triumph." Gill pictures the two "about their proper business, of husbandry."

On the crucial question — is the one "taken" saved or destroyed? — the witnesses divide. Barnes leaves it open: "taken may mean either to be taken away from the danger, i.e. rescued, as Lot was (Luke 17:28-29), or to be taken away by death. Probably the latter." Wesley reads "taken" as saved: "Into God's immediate protection; and one is left — to share the common calamities." Ellicott weighs the Greek and decides for salvation: "the use of the Greek word in other passages (Matthew 1:20; 12:45; John 14:3) is in favour of the former interpretation" — the man "taken" is "received into fellowship with Christ, while the other is abandoned." The constant teaching, he adds, is "that the day of judgment will be, as by an inevitable law, a day of separation, according to the diversity of character which may exist in the midst of the closest fellowship in outward life."

The Reformed Calvin links it to Luke's added warnings — "remember Lot's wife" (Genesis 19:26), who looked back and "gave the lie to the heavenly oracle"; believers must "rise cheerfully towards heaven," not letting "the ties by which human beings are bound to each other in the world... hinder or retard the godly." Even out of "a single couple," one may be taken and one left, "that those who are already prepared may not waste their time in waiting for their companions" — though "the sacred bond of piety will cause a believing wife to cleave to a believing husband." Cyril of Jerusalem, here on-topic, comforts the lowly: "the Judge is no respecter of persons... Though thou be in the field, the Angels shall take thee; think not that He will take the landowners, and leave thee the husbandman." The witnesses agree the verse teaches a sudden, character-based separation; they differ chiefly on whether "taken" is the lot of the saved or the doomed.
41:
"Two women shall be grinding at the mill; the one shall be taken, and the other left." The witnesses treat this as a companion image to v.40, extending the lesson of sudden separation to another humble scene of daily labour. The grammatical-historical voices supply the realia. Barnes and Robertson describe the hand-mill: two stones about two feet across, the lower fixed, the upper turned by a handle, worked by two women sitting opposite — "a very laborious employment" (Exodus 11:5; Isaiah 47:2; Judges 16:21). Gill notes the word "grinding" is feminine, "the work of women," rightly supplied as "two women." Ellicott observes the image, like the fig tree, "may have been suggested by what was present to our Lord's view at the time — the Mount of Olives might well have presented to His gaze... the two labourers in the field, the two women at the mill"; and he recalls that grinding was "the crowning degradation of male captives taken in battle" (Judges 16:21; Lamentations 5:13).

The meaning, the witnesses agree, is identical to v.40: "of two persons sitting near to each other, one shall be taken, and the other left. The calamity would be sudden, and would come upon them before they were aware" (Barnes). Wesley simply notes grinding "was then a common employment of women." Cyril of Jerusalem, again on-topic, presses the comfort to the lowliest: "Though thou be of compulsion put to grind, whether thou be man or woman... yet He who by His might bringeth out them that are bound" will not despise thee — "the Judge is no respecter of persons."

The pairing of the two scenes — two men in the field, two women at the mill — is deliberate, spanning the labours of both sexes and the whole texture of ordinary life. In each case the separation cuts through the closest outward fellowship: two doing the same work, side by side, yet one taken and one left. The thin comment of the witnesses matches the brevity of the verse, which adds no new doctrine but reinforces the warning of v.40 by repetition and variation. The point is the inwardness and suddenness of the division: no outward circumstance — not the field, not the mill, not the company one keeps — determines one's lot, but only the diversity of character that the coming of the Son of Man will lay bare. Two may share the same toil and the same roof, and be parted forever in a moment.
42:
"Watch therefore: for ye know not what hour your Lord doth come." The witnesses receive this as the practical heart of the discourse — the uncertainty of the time is given precisely to keep us watchful. Jerome: Christ "shews that it was not expedient for the Apostles to know, that being ignorant they might live in perpetual expectation of His coming"; and he notes Christ says "Because ye know not," not "we," "shewing that He Himself is not ignorant." Gregory defines watching: "to keep the eyes open, and looking out for the true light... to cast away the darkness of sloth and negligence"; if the master had watched, "he would have been secure from the thief," so "the Lord would have the last hour unknown, that it might always be in suspense, and that... we might never be unprepared." Chrysostom rebukes "such as have less care for their souls than they have of guarding their money against an expected thief."

Augustine widens it to every believer and every death: Christ said "Watch" not to the apostles only "but to those who came after them, and to us... That day comes to each one of us, when it comes to him to go out of the world, such as he shall be judged"; and "foolish are all they who profess to know the day of the end of the world." The Alexandrian Origen reads the thief allegorically as "the Devil" or "every contrary doctrine which enters the soul of the unwary."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices draw the discipline. Clarke: "Be always on your guard... The followers of Christ took this advice, and therefore they escaped; the miserable Jews rejected it, and were destroyed." Robertson notes Jesus reinforces the point with "a half dozen parables." Calvin presses Luke's fuller form — watch, take heed lest the heart be overcharged, and pray — and turns the uncertainty into an argument against sloth: "the uncertainty as to the time of Christ's coming — which almost all treat as an encouragement to sloth — ought to be felt by us to be an excitement to attention and watchfulness. God intended that it should be hidden from us, for the express purpose that we may keep diligent watch without the relaxation of a single hour." Henry: "To watch for Christ's coming is to maintain that temper of mind which we would be willing that our Lord should find us in." The witnesses agree without dissent: the hidden hour is a summons to perpetual readiness.
43:
"But know this, that if the goodman of the house had known in what watch the thief would come, he would have watched, and would not have suffered his house to be broken up." The witnesses read this brief parable of the thief as an argument for constant vigilance, drawn from common prudence. Barnes: "If a man knew the hour... when a robber would come, he would be ready for him. So you know not the exact hour, but you know it is near"; the Lord "will come suddenly, as a thief comes, without giving previous warning" (1 Thessalonians 5:2; 2 Peter 3:10; Revelation 3:3; 16:15). He explains the terms: "goodman" is the householder, "thief" properly "a robber" who comes "by housebreaking," and "broken up" means "broken into — either by the doors or windows."

Clarke (citing Wakefield) draws the lesson: "As a master of a family who expected a thief at any time of the night would take care to be awake... so do ye, who know that the Son of man will come. Though the day and hour be uncertain, continue always in a state of watchfulness." Ellicott supplies the literary background, noting these verses (Matthew 24:43-51) appear "almost verbatim in another discourse" (Luke 12:42 ff.), and that the thief-image "passed into the proverbial saying, that the day of the Lord would come as a thief in the night, quoted by St. Paul in 1 Thessalonians 5:2." Robertson explains "broken through" as "digged through the tile roof or under the floor (dirt in the poorer houses)."

The Reformed Calvin frames the whole as an exhortation to watch: "if any person shall hear that robbers are prowling in the night, fear and suspicion will not allow him to sleep. Since, therefore, we are informed that Christ's coming will be sudden and unexpected, like that of a robber... there is no excuse for our indolence." He sharpens the stakes beyond the thief's usual threat: "there is reason to dread not only a breach of the wall, and a loss of our property, but a deadly wound to ruin our soul, unless we are on our guard."

The witnesses agree: the figure argues from the lesser to the greater. Every householder, forewarned that a thief is abroad, would keep awake to guard his goods; how much more should the believer, knowing that his Lord will come suddenly and at an unknown hour, keep spiritual watch — for what is at stake is not property but the soul. The very suddenness that makes the thief's coming dreadful is the suddenness Christ attaches to his own advent, turning a homely picture of nocturnal danger into a summons to unceasing readiness. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here belongs to the parable of the two sons, not this verse.)
44:
"Therefore be ye also ready: for in such an hour as ye think not the Son of man cometh." The witnesses receive this as the conclusion of the thief-parable and the summons to constant preparedness. Gill defines the readiness required: not merely "a readiness to do the will and work of God," but, in the historical context, "a preparedness to meet the Lord in the way of his judgments, when desolating judgments are coming on the earth, such as these in Jerusalem; by faith and trust in the power, providence, and care of God; by humiliation before him, and resignation to his will" — and, applied to "a future state after death," a readiness that "lies not in a dependence on the absolute mercy of God" alone nor in "external humiliation," but in true faith.

Barnes brings in Luke's parallel exhortations: Christ "charged them to pray always, that they might be accounted worthy to escape those things... and to stand before the Son of man" (Luke 21:36), and to "take heed... lest their hearts be overcharged with surfeiting, and drunkenness, and the cares of this life" (Luke 21:34) — "things improper if there were no judgment, peculiarly mad and wicked when the judgment is near." Ellicott draws the methodological lesson: the words show "that even the signs which were to be as the budding of the fig-tree... were intended only to rouse the faithful to watchfulness, not to enable men to fix the times and the seasons." The "apparent destiny of failure which has attended on all attempts" to compute the date "might have been avoided had men been more careful to restrain... their efforts after knowledge within the limits of the knowable."

Robertson states it bluntly: "It is useless to set the day and hour for Christ's coming. It is folly to neglect it." The same figure of the thief "will be used also by Paul concerning the unexpectedness of Christ's second coming" (1 Thessalonians 5:2). The Reformed Calvin: "since we are expressly forewarned that we must always watch, lest he come upon us when asleep... there is no excuse for our indolence; more especially since there is reason to dread... a deadly wound to ruin our soul. Though the last judgment be delayed for a long time, yet it hangs over us every hour."

The witnesses agree on the verse's plain and pressing force: because the hour is unknown and the coming sudden, the only safety is to be always ready. Readiness is not a matter of calculating times — that effort is condemned as both futile and presumptuous — but of a settled posture of faith, watchfulness, and prayer, such that whenever the Lord comes, in judgment on a city or in the final advent or in the hour of death, he finds his servant prepared.
45:
"Who then is a faithful and wise servant, whom his lord hath made ruler over his household, to give them meat in due season?" The witnesses read this parable of the steward as addressed especially to ministers, the watchfulness just enjoined now applied to those set over Christ's household. The Latin fathers make the application explicit. Hilary: Christ "adds a special charge to the rulers of the people, that is, the Bishops," calling faithful the one "obedient to his Lord's command, by the seasonableness of his teaching dispensing the word of life." The Gloss: "rare indeed is such faithful servant... feeding Christ's sheep not for lucre but for love of Christ." Origen distinguishes the two virtues: "to give food in due season calls for prudence; not to take away the food of the needy requires faithfulness," warning against devouring "that which belongs to the widows" and against giving "intellectual and spiritual food" indiscriminately rather than "according as each has need."

The grammatical-historical voices identify the servant as the Christian minister. Barnes: "a faithful Christian, a servant of God, or a teacher of religion"; "meat in due season" means feeding "the flock of God... as they need it" (John 21:15-17; Acts 20:28). Ellicott notes "wise" is the word for "moral wisdom which adapts means to ends," and that there is "an art of spiritual dietetics, which requires tact and discernment as well as faithfulness" — the minister discerning "not only the right kind of food, but the right season for giving it" (milk or "strong meat," 1 Peter 2:2; Hebrews 5:12). Clarke lists six duties of the minister: appointed by Christ, a servant not master, faithful, prudent, feeding the family true nourishment, "and this in its season."

The Reformed Calvin (following Luke, where Peter asks whether the warning is general or for the Twelve) makes it a fortiori: "if each of the common people ought to watch, much less ought it to be endured that the apostles should be asleep." The higher the rank, "the heavier was the burden"; stewards "whose office embraces the care of the whole family ought to go much farther" than ordinary servants, excelling "by extraordinary fidelity and wisdom." Wesley: "Which of you aspires after this character?" The witnesses agree: the parable turns the general call to watch into a particular charge on Christ's stewards to feed his people faithfully and wisely, in season — the standard by which they will be judged at his coming.
46:
"Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing." The brief verse draws short comment, the witnesses being Gill, Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott. Gill marks the several "comings" in view: "whether in a way of judgment, as against Jerusalem; or at death, when he comes to remove him out of time into eternity; or at the day of judgment, when he, the righteous judge, will give the crown of righteousness." Clarke locates the blessedness simply: "His blessedness consists in his master's approbation."

Ellicott draws out the wider application that the parable invites. Taken "in their letter," the words "seem to refer only to those who shall thus be found at the time of the final Advent." But "Christian insight has rightly given them a wider application. As there are days of the Lord in the history of churches and nations, so the Lord comes to men in the crises of their individual lives; and one such coming is that day of death which closes the trial-time of their earthly life, and brings them into the presence of the Judge." The blessing therefore belongs not only to the servant alive at the last day, but to every faithful steward whom the Lord finds at his post whenever and however he comes.

The emphasis falls on "so doing" — the servant is blessed not for what he professes or intends, but for being found actually engaged in the work committed to him: feeding the household in due season (v.45). The blessedness is the approbation of the returning master, the "Well done, good and faithful servant" that the parallel parable of the talents makes explicit (Matthew 25:21). The thinness of the witnesses' comment matches the verse, which simply pronounces the reward of the faithful steward before turning, in vv.48-51, to the fearful contrast of the evil servant. The point is plain and searching: it is not enough to have been appointed to office, nor to have begun well; the blessing is for the one whom the sudden coming finds still faithfully "so doing" — diligent, watchful, and at his work — at the very moment the Lord arrives. The whole weight of the parable, and of the discourse, presses toward that question: when he comes, in what state will he find us?
47:
"Verily I say unto you, That he shall make him ruler over all his goods." The witnesses read this as the reward of the faithful steward — promotion and enlarged trust. Gill ties it to the several comings: such reward awaits "all such wise, faithful, diligent, and industrious servants." Barnes is careful to keep the figure from over-literal application to ecclesiastical rank: "This does not mean that ministers shall have a higher rank or office, but is a circumstance of the parable, designed to show the effect of faithfulness. Faithful servants of Christ shall be rewarded... by his approbation, and by the rewards of the heavenly world."

Clarke, by contrast, presses a high view of the minister's reward: "He shall receive from God a power to dispense all the blessings of the new covenant; and his word shall ever be accompanied with the demonstration of the Holy Ghost to the hearts of all that hear it." He adds the warning that "much of a preacher's usefulness may be lost by his unfaithfulness." Ellicott draws a remarkable inference about the continuity of service beyond this life: the words are "noteworthy as among the indications that the work of the faithful servant does not cease, either after his own removal from his earthly labour, or even after the final consummation of the kingdom. Over and above the joy of the beatific vision... there will still be a work to be done, analogous to that which has been the man's training here, and in it there will be scope for all the faculties and energies that have been thus disciplined and developed" (Luke 19:17).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which completes the picture of the faithful servant's blessedness (v.46) with the specific reward: enlarged authority, "ruler over all his goods." The witnesses agree on the substance — fidelity in a trust is rewarded with a greater trust — while differing in how far to press the detail. Barnes treats "ruler over all his goods" as merely a feature of the story illustrating reward; Ellicott finds in it a genuine glimpse of heaven as a realm not of idle rest but of continued, joyful service, the faithful steward's earthly training fitting him for larger work in the world to come. Either way, the promise crowns the parable's call to faithfulness: the servant who feeds the household well in his lord's absence will, at his lord's coming, be entrusted with all. The reward is proportioned to the fidelity, and it answers the discourse's repeated summons to be found watching and working when the Lord returns.
48:
"But and if that evil servant shall say in his heart, My lord delayeth his coming." The witnesses identify here the root of the steward's corruption — a secret unbelief in the nearness of reckoning. Gill notes such a one "may have gifts and talents, yet [is] destitute of the grace of God," in "the highest post and office in the church" yet graceless. Barnes: "Say in his heart — secretly suppose... Will not return in a long time; or perhaps may not at all." Clarke lists "three characters of a bad minister," the first being that "he has little or no faith in the speedy coming of Christ... It may be he does not outwardly profess this, but he says it in his heart, and God searches his heart."

Ellicott and Robertson connect the temper directly to apostolic prophecy. Ellicott: "the temper described is identical with that portrayed in 2 Peter 3:3-4. The words are memorable as implying the prescience, even in the immediate context of words that indicate nearness, that there would be what to men would seem delay." Those who, like Peter, rightly understood the delay "would continue watchful, but the selfish and ungodly would be tempted by it to forget that Christ comes to men in more senses and more ways than one. The tyranny and sensuality which have at times stained the annals of the Church of Christ have had their origin in this forgetfulness, that though the final coming may be delayed, the Judge is ever near, even at the doors" (James 5:9). Robertson: "Within a generation scoffers will be asking where is the promise of the coming of Christ (2 Peter 3:4). They will forget that God's clock is not like our clock."

The Reformed Calvin traces the whole evil to this presumption of delay: "they trust to a longer delay, and thus of their own accord involve themselves in darkness. They imagine that the day when they must render an account will never come; and, under the pretext of Christ's absence, they promise themselves that they will remain unpunished." The remedy is to "constantly make our minds familiar with the thought of that last and sudden coming of the Lord, the neglect of which leads the reprobate to indulge in wickedness." Calvin notes the servant is not "merely dissolute" but "rises up in an outrageous manner to disturb the whole house." Wesley: "Now evil, having put away faith and a good conscience." The witnesses agree: the secret thought "my lord delayeth" is the seed of all the tyranny and sensuality the next verse describes — practical unbelief breeding practical wickedness.
49:
"And shall begin to smite his fellowservants, and to eat and drink with the drunken." The witnesses see the evil servant's secret unbelief (v.48) issuing in two outward sins: tyranny over fellow-servants and sensual self-indulgence. Gill applies the smiting to spiritual oppression in the church: "abusing the power lodged in him, usurping a dominion over their faith, and imposing on their consciences things which Christ has never commanded; vexing and burdening them with trifling rites and ceremonies... or persecuting the saints" who will not conform to his way.

Barnes reads it of unfaithful ministers: "the conduct of a wicked servant who, supposing he would not be called to account, and abusing his authority, gave himself up to oppression, carousing, and debauchery. It is designed to represent the conduct of ministers who are unfaithful, overbearing, and who abuse their trust in the church." The two faults are characteristic: cruelty toward those under him, and dissipation in the company of the worldly. The one who should feed the household (v.45) instead beats it; the one who should be sober and watchful instead feasts "with the drunken."

The verse stands in deliberate contrast to the faithful steward. Where the good servant gave the household "meat in due season," the evil servant takes the food and turns on the family; where the good servant watched, the evil one carouses. Both sins flow from the same source — the conviction that the master's coming is far off and the reckoning will never come. Tyranny and sensuality are the twin fruits of practical atheism in a position of trust: having dismissed the Judge from his thoughts, the servant treats the household as his own to abuse and the master's goods as his own to squander.

The thin comment of the witnesses matches the verse, which simply itemizes the evil servant's conduct before the sentence falls in vv.50-51. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here, citing Jeremiah 26 and 2 Chronicles 24 and Matthew 23:37, belongs to the persecution of the prophets, not directly to this verse, though it aptly recalls that the smiting of God's servants is an old sin.) The witnesses agree: the man who says in his heart that his Lord delays will soon show, by oppression and indulgence, that he never truly believed his Lord would come at all — and it is precisely such a one whom the sudden coming will overtake and condemn.
50:
"The lord of that servant shall come in a day when he looketh not for him, and in an hour that he is not aware of." The witnesses read this as the sudden overtaking of the careless servant, the very suddenness he had presumed against. Gill notes the lord comes upon one who is his servant "not by redemption and grace, but by creation and profession" — a nominal servant, never truly the Lord's. Barnes simply restates the force: the master comes "in a day when he looked not for him" (1 Thessalonians 5:3; Revelation 3:3), the unexpectedness that the whole discourse has pressed.

Clarke draws the correspondence between the three sins and three punishments with which the chapter closes. The first punishment answers the first sin: "a sudden death, and the weight of God's judgments falling upon him, without a moment to avert it: this answers to his infidelity and forgetfulness. He shall come in a day in which he looked not for him." The very thought that licensed his wickedness — "my lord delayeth his coming" (v.48) — is refuted by the event: the lord comes precisely when not expected. The servant's calculation of delay becomes the instrument of his ruin, for it left him unprepared at the one moment that mattered.

The brevity of comment matches the verse, which is the hinge between the evil servant's conduct (vv.48-49) and his doom (v.51). The witnesses agree on its plain sense: the coming that the faithful servant watched for and the evil servant dismissed arrives suddenly for both, but with opposite results. For the watchful it is blessing (v.46); for the careless it is catastrophe. The unexpectedness is not arbitrary but just — the servant had been warned, had been given his trust, and had chosen to live as though no reckoning would come; the sudden arrival simply exposes the truth of his heart at the worst possible time for him.

This is the sober counterpart to the chapter's repeated comfort. The same hidden hour that keeps the faithful watchful and ready (v.42) becomes, for the presumptuous, the snare that catches them in their sin. The Lord's clock is not man's clock (as Robertson said on v.48); the servant who reads the delay as license has misread it fatally, and the day he thought far off stands suddenly at the door. The verse thus seals the warning of the whole discourse: because no one knows the hour, the only safety is to live every hour as the faithful steward did — and the only certainty for the evil servant is that the hour will find him out.
51:
"And shall cut him asunder, and appoint him his portion with the hypocrites: there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth." The witnesses receive this severe sentence as the doom of the unfaithful servant, and they explain both the dreadful image and its meaning. On "cut asunder," the grammatical-historical voices document an ancient mode of execution. Barnes: "anciently practised — sometimes by the sword, sometimes by saws... among the Chaldeans (Daniel 2:5; 3:29) and among the Hebrews (1 Samuel 15:33; Hebrews 11:37)... not perhaps to be taken literally, but signifies that the wicked servant should be severely punished." Clarke supplies travellers' and historians' parallels — Herodotus, the Turkish punishment for manslaughter — noting "this is the very same punishment, and for the same offense... the killing of a fellow servant."

Several read it figuratively of separation. Jerome: "not execution by the sword, but that he shall sever him from the company of the saints, and appoint him his portion with the hypocrites." Ellicott finds the figure "chosen for its figurative fitness. The man had been a hypocrite, double-minded, trying to serve two masters, and his Lord, with the sharp sword of judgment, smites through the false, apparent unity of his life, and reveals its duplicity." The portion "with the hypocrites" links this servant to the woes of the previous chapter; he is unmasked as one who, beneath the office of a steward, was a pretender all along.

The puritan Henry expounds the "two dreadful words, death and damnation": "His Lord shall cut him off from the land of the living... shall cut him asunder, that is, part body and soul, send the body to the grave... and the soul to hell. The wicked servant divided himself between God and the world, Christ and Belial, his profession and his lusts; justly therefore will he thus be divided." Clarke completes his threefold correspondence: the separation answers to the abuse of authority, and the "weeping and gnashing of teeth" to the voluptuous life.

Clarke closes the chapter with a long reflection on the precise fulfilment of the whole prophecy in the fall of Jerusalem — attested by Josephus, Roman historians, "triumphal arches, coins, medals," and the continued dispersion of the Jews — arguing that he who "so minutely fulfilled the threatenings of his justice" will as surely "fulfil the promises of his grace." The witnesses agree: the discourse that began with the temple's doom ends with the doom of the false servant, and both stand as warnings that the Lord, whose word shall not pass away (v.35), will come, will reckon, and will not be mocked.
